claims.text: On September 1, 1859 astronomer Richard Carrington was observing the sun when he saw a sunspot about ten times the size of Earth emit a flash of intense white light, the first recorded observation of a major coronal mass ejection.

evidence.quote:
d out why
[Music]
on september 1st astronomer richard
carrington was studying the sun when he
noticed something unusual at about 11
a.m he was observing a huge

claims.rationale: Richard Carrington's September 1, 1859 white-light flare observation at his Redhill (UK) observatory is extensively documented and is the canonical first recorded solar flare observation. The Earth-sized sunspot and CME framing match modern consensus.

claims.text: About 17 hours after the 1859 flare, the Carrington-event CME hit Earth and produced auroras visible as far south as Miami, Cuba, and Colombia and north of Brisbane in the southern hemisphere.

evidence.quote:
17 hours later
the night sky in north america lit up
like the day aurora borealis typically
seen near the north pole were visible in
miami and cuba and as far so

claims.rationale: Contemporary newspaper reports document auroras at Carribbean and South American latitudes during the Carrington event. The 17-hour transit time matches the observed September 1–2, 1859 sequence.

claims.text: During the 1859 storm telegraph lines in Europe and North America shorted, threw sparks, and in some cases burst into flames, injuring telegraph operators with electric shocks.

evidence.quote:
around the world telegraph lines
were shorting out in europe and north
america equipment was throwing sparks
and some burst into flames telegraph
operators wer

claims.rationale: Newspapers from September 1859 describe telegraph office fires, sparks, and operator electric shocks, along with the famous anomaly of telegraphs continuing to transmit with power supplies disconnected. Well-documented.

claims.text: The Kew Observatory in London recorded a major geomagnetic disturbance simultaneous with Carrington's flare observation, establishing the first correlation between solar flares and Earth's magnetic field.

evidence.quote:
the eruption the q
observatory in london reported a large
magnetic disturbance in the ionosphere
this allowed scientists to correctly
link geomagnetic storms wi

claims.rationale: Kew Observatory did record a magnetometer trace coincident with Carrington's flare. This simultaneous observation is the foundational datum linking solar activity to geomagnetic storms and is standard in space-weather histories.

claims.text: Coronal mass ejections occur two or three times a day on average, but once or twice every hundred years the sun produces superstorms capable of reaching Earth.

evidence.quote:
sun
on average cmes happen two or three
times a day but once or twice every
hundred years the sun creates super
storms that hit the earth and when they
do the

claims.rationale: Average CME rates of one-to-several per day match SOHO-era observations. The 'once or twice every hundred years' superstorm rate is a common heuristic but the true return period for Carrington-class events is uncertain (estimates range roughly 100–500 years).

claims.text: Ice core samples show powerful solar storms struck Earth multiple times between 7000 and 5000 BC, and a 774 AD event (the Miyake event) produced the largest carbon-14 spike ever recorded.

evidence.quote:
e ice core samples
show powerful solar storms hit the earth
several times between 7000 and 5000 bc
in 774 a.d an extreme solar storm called
the miyake event cau

claims.rationale: The 774 AD Miyake event was first identified in tree-ring carbon-14 records by Miyake et al. 2012 and subsequently cross-confirmed in polar ice beryllium-10. Ice-core evidence of Holocene superstorms including the 7000–5000 BC window is supported.

claims.text: A 993 AD solar storm left a carbon-14 spike in tree trunks that archaeologists use to date ancient wood, including to confirm Norse arrival in North America roughly 500 years before Columbus.

evidence.quote:
rded in 993
a.d a storm left evidence in tree trunks
that archaeologists still use to date
ancient wood materials and this is one
of the ways they confirmed tha

claims.rationale: The 992–993 AD Miyake event was used by Kuitems et al. (Nature 2021) to precisely date Norse occupation at L'Anse aux Meadows to AD 1021. The '500 years before Columbus' framing is accurate.

claims.text: In 1989 a solar storm knocked out power in Quebec province for nine hours, disrupted Russian radio signals, caused satellite drift, and induced sensor malfunctions on the Space Shuttle Discovery.

evidence.quote:
ally
happened in 1989 the earth was hit by a
solar storm that disrupted radio signals
across russia oh the russians had to be
annoyed oh they were at first they

claims.rationale: The March 13, 1989 geomagnetic storm caused the Hydro-Québec grid collapse for nine hours and produced documented satellite and shuttle anomalies. Historically solid.

claims.text: The 2003 Halloween solar storm put millions of people in the US and Canada in darkness for 12 hours.

evidence.quote:
blackout for nine hours in 2003
another storm put millions of people in
the u.s and canada in darkness for 12
hours and the solar storms of 1989 and
2003 were a

claims.rationale: The October 2003 Halloween storms did cause a Sweden grid outage and widespread satellite damage, but the specific claim of 'millions in US/Canada darkness for 12 hours' overstates the North American impact, which was more limited than the 1989 Quebec event.

claims.text: In July 2012 the largest CME in a century missed Earth by nine days; had it hit, a quarter of Earth's population would have been without power for weeks and US damage alone would have cost trillions.

evidence.quote:
Music]
in july 2012 the largest cma in a
century missed the earth by nine days
that was the luckiest thing to happen to
our species in hundreds of years if that

claims.rationale: The July 23, 2012 CME was a Carrington-class event that crossed Earth's orbit nine days after Earth had passed the impact point. Observed by STEREO-A. NASA risk estimates match the video's framing. The trillions-dollar figure is a reasonable upper bound.

claims.text: NASA's STEREO spacecraft launched in 2006 can spot a CME almost immediately, but cannot determine the CME's magnetic field orientation, which is the key determinant of Earth impact severity.

evidence.quote:
stressful well
nasa's solar terrestrial relations
observatory spacecraft called stereo
launched in 2006 and it can spot a cme
almost immediately
but oh no but t

claims.rationale: STEREO-A and STEREO-B launched October 25, 2006 for heliospheric imaging. STEREO coronagraphs detect CMEs but do not directly resolve their magnetic-field orientation — this is accurate.

claims.text: The Advanced Composition Explorer (ACE), stationed about 900,000 miles from Earth, can measure a CME's magnetic field orientation and gives roughly 30 minutes of warning time before impact.

evidence.quote:
w the advanced
composition explorer sits about 900 000
miles from the earth and this satellite
can detect the cme's magnetic field
which gives us how much time

claims.rationale: ACE orbits L1 at roughly 1.5 million km (~930,000 miles) from Earth and does measure solar-wind magnetic field upstream. The 30-minute warning is a standard reference figure, depending on CME speed.

claims.text: On average, there is a roughly four percent chance of a severe solar storm in any given year and a 0.7 percent chance of a Carrington-level event.

evidence.quote:
rage there's a four
percent chance of a severe solar storm
in any given year and a 0.7 percent
chance of a carrington level event
but every 11 years the sun ent

claims.rationale: Probability estimates for Carrington-level events vary widely in the literature. Pete Riley's 2012 paper famously estimated ~12% per decade (close to this 0.7% per year), but subsequent analyses span a factor of several. The numbers here are plausible but uncertain.

claims.text: Solar cycle 25 began in 2019 and is expected to peak in 2025, raising annual odds to 28% for severe storms and 4% for a Carrington-class superstorm.

evidence.quote:
re currently in solar cycle 25
which began in 2019 and is expected to
peak in 2025. during the solar maximum
chances of a severe solar storm go to 28
and chance

claims.rationale: Solar Cycle 25 did begin around December 2019 and is peaking around 2024–2025. The specific 28% / 4% odds during maximum appear to be a rough reformulation of Riley-style statistics scaled to cycle-maximum periods — order-of-magnitude plausible, not precise.

claims.text: Governments have not diverted sufficient resources toward solar-storm preparedness because a four-percent annual risk is insufficiently alarming, leaving individual preparedness (food, water, thermal blankets, radios) as the practical defense.

evidence.quote:
ed in recent years a
four percent chance is just not scary
enough for governments to take the
necessary action we need more resources
diverted to research more s

claims.rationale: Government under-investment in space-weather hardening is a legitimate critique echoed by NOAA and NASA advisory panels. The implicit prescription for personal prepping is an opinion (solar storms differ from other disasters in that no amount of personal gear prevents grid collapse).
